questions.question_text: Which statement best describes electric circuits in electricity: magnetic and heating effects?

options.text: Cells, switches, wires, and loads perform different roles in an electric circuit.
An electric circuit is a complete conducting path through which electric current can flow.
Conductors allow electric current to pass relatively easily, while insulators resist its flow.
Electric current can produce heat when it passes through resistance in a material.

questions.explanation: An electric circuit is a complete conducting path through which electric current can flow.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with electric circuits?

options.text: Copper wire conducts, while plastic covering helps insulate it.
A switch controls the path while a lamp changes electrical energy into light and heat.
A cell, wires, and a closed switch can light a lamp.
An electric heater uses a resistant element to become hot.

questions.explanation: A cell, wires, and a closed switch can light a lamp.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes electric circuits from a nearby idea?

options.text: Heating is an energy conversion effect, not evidence that current is being created.
A source supplies energy, a conductor provides a path, and a load uses energy.
The same object can have conducting and insulating parts for different safety roles.
A closed conducting path is necessary; merely placing components nearby is not enough.

questions.explanation: A closed conducting path is necessary; merely placing components nearby is not enough.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of electric circuits?

options.text: Trace the complete path from one terminal of the cell and back to the other.
Represent a circuit using standard symbols before assembling it.
Test an unknown material only with a low-voltage classroom circuit.
Select a properly rated appliance and keep flammable materials away from its heating element.

questions.explanation: Trace the complete path from one terminal of the cell and back to the other.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about electric circuits?

options.text: A switch is not an energy source.
A circuit with a gap cannot keep the lamp glowing.
A shiny appearance does not by itself prove that a material conducts.
A hot wire is not automatically safe to touch.

questions.explanation: A circuit with a gap cannot keep the lamp glowing.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining electric circuits. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: Conductors allow electric current to pass relatively easily, while insulators resist its flow.
Cells, switches, wires, and loads perform different roles in an electric circuit.
An electric circuit is a complete conducting path through which electric current can flow.
Electric current can produce heat when it passes through resistance in a material.

questions.explanation: An electric circuit is a complete conducting path through which electric current can flow.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about electric circuits?

options.text: An electric heater uses a resistant element to become hot.
A switch controls the path while a lamp changes electrical energy into light and heat.
Copper wire conducts, while plastic covering helps insulate it.
A cell, wires, and a closed switch can light a lamp.

options.text: A source supplies energy, a conductor provides a path, and a load uses energy.
A closed conducting path is necessary; merely placing components nearby is not enough.
The same object can have conducting and insulating parts for different safety roles.

options.text: Test an unknown material only with a low-voltage classroom circuit.
Represent a circuit using standard symbols before assembling it.
Trace the complete path from one terminal of the cell and back to the other.
Select a properly rated appliance and keep flammable materials away from its heating element.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes circuit components in electricity: magnetic and heating effects?

options.text: An electric current can produce a magnetic field around a conductor.
Conductors allow electric current to pass relatively easily, while insulators resist its flow.
Electric current can produce heat when it passes through resistance in a material.
Cells, switches, wires, and loads perform different roles in an electric circuit.

questions.explanation: Cells, switches, wires, and loads perform different roles in an electric circuit.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with circuit components?

options.text: A switch controls the path while a lamp changes electrical energy into light and heat.
Copper wire conducts, while plastic covering helps insulate it.
An electric heater uses a resistant element to become hot.
A current-carrying coil can attract an iron object.

questions.explanation: A switch controls the path while a lamp changes electrical energy into light and heat.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes circuit components from a nearby idea?

options.text: The same object can have conducting and insulating parts for different safety roles.
A source supplies energy, a conductor provides a path, and a load uses energy.
Heating is an energy conversion effect, not evidence that current is being created.
The magnetic effect comes from current in the circuit, not from the wire simply being made of metal.

questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of circuit components?

options.text: Select a properly rated appliance and keep flammable materials away from its heating element.
Test an unknown material only with a low-voltage classroom circuit.
Represent a circuit using standard symbols before assembling it.
Increase the useful magnetic effect with a coil and a suitable iron core.

questions.explanation: Represent a circuit using standard symbols before assembling it.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about circuit components?

options.text: A disconnected wire does not produce the same magnetic effect as a current-carrying one.
A shiny appearance does not by itself prove that a material conducts.
A hot wire is not automatically safe to touch.
A switch is not an energy source.

questions.explanation: A switch is not an energy source.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes conductors and insulators in electricity: magnetic and heating effects?

options.text: Electric current can produce heat when it passes through resistance in a material.
Conductors allow electric current to pass relatively easily, while insulators resist its flow.
An electric current can produce a magnetic field around a conductor.
An electromagnet is a temporary magnet produced by current through a coil, often around an iron core.

questions.explanation: Conductors allow electric current to pass relatively easily, while insulators resist its flow.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with conductors and insulators?

options.text: A current-carrying coil can attract an iron object.
An electric heater uses a resistant element to become hot.
Copper wire conducts, while plastic covering helps insulate it.
A scrap-yard lifting device can use an electromagnet to pick up iron.

questions.explanation: Copper wire conducts, while plastic covering helps insulate it.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes conductors and insulators from a nearby idea?

options.text: An electromagnet can be switched and its strength can be changed, unlike a simple permanent magnet.
Heating is an energy conversion effect, not evidence that current is being created.
The magnetic effect comes from current in the circuit, not from the wire simply being made of metal.
The same object can have conducting and insulating parts for different safety roles.

questions.explanation: The same object can have conducting and insulating parts for different safety roles.

options.text: Test an unknown material only with a low-voltage classroom circuit.
Select a properly rated appliance and keep flammable materials away from its heating element.
Increase the useful magnetic effect with a coil and a suitable iron core.
Use an insulated coil, a soft iron core, and a low-voltage source for a model.

Electric current can produce heat when it passes through resistance in a material.

options.text: An electric heater uses a resistant element to become hot.
A current-carrying coil can attract an iron object.
A scrap-yard lifting device can use an electromagnet to pick up iron.
The motor in a toy fan turns its blades.

questions.explanation: An electric heater uses a resistant element to become hot.

A current-carrying coil can attract an iron object.

options.text: A fuse is a safety device, not a switch for routine operation.

A motor produces motion, while a generator uses motion to produce electrical energy.
The magnetic effect comes from current in the circuit, not from the wire simply being made of metal.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes electric motors in electricity: magnetic and heating effects?

options.text: A fuse protects a circuit by melting and opening the path when excessive current flows.
An electric motor converts electrical energy into mechanical motion using magnetic forces.
In a series circuit components share one path, while a parallel circuit provides separate branches.
Electrical appliances transfer electrical energy into useful forms such as light, heat, sound, or motion.

questions.explanation: An electric motor converts electrical energy into mechanical motion using magnetic forces.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with electric motors?

options.text: Two lamps in parallel can continue to receive a path if one branch opens.
A fuse can disconnect a circuit during a short circuit or overload.
The motor in a toy fan turns its blades.
A mixer converts electrical energy mainly into mechanical motion and sound.

questions.explanation: The motor in a toy fan turns its blades.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes electric motors from a nearby idea?

options.text: Energy use depends on both appliance power and the time it operates.
A fuse is a safety device, not a switch for routine operation.
Series and parallel arrangements change how current paths and component behaviour are distributed.
A motor produces motion, while a generator uses motion to produce electrical energy.

questions.explanation: A motor produces motion, while a generator uses motion to produce electrical energy.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of electric motors?

options.text: Identify the motor as the component that drives a rotating part in an appliance.
Use the correct fuse rating and never replace a fuse with wire.
Choose parallel branches when independent operation of lamps is needed.
Switch off an appliance when it is not needed and use an efficient option.

questions.explanation: Identify the motor as the component that drives a rotating part in an appliance.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about electric motors?

options.text: A thicker wire in place of a fuse removes protection rather than improving it.
A motor does not run without a suitable circuit and energy source.
Adding more lamps in series does not usually make every lamp brighter.
An appliance that is switched on is using energy even if the useful output is small.

questions.explanation: A motor does not run without a suitable circuit and energy source.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining electric motors. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: In a series circuit components share one path, while a parallel circuit provides separate branches.

options.text: A mixer converts electrical energy mainly into mechanical motion and sound.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes fuses and electrical safety in electricity: magnetic and heating effects?

options.text: An electric circuit is a complete conducting path through which electric current can flow.
In a series circuit components share one path, while a parallel circuit provides separate branches.
Electrical appliances transfer electrical energy into useful forms such as light, heat, sound, or motion.
A fuse protects a circuit by melting and opening the path when excessive current flows.

questions.explanation: A fuse protects a circuit by melting and opening the path when excessive current flows.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with fuses and electrical safety?

options.text: A fuse can disconnect a circuit during a short circuit or overload.
Two lamps in parallel can continue to receive a path if one branch opens.
A mixer converts electrical energy mainly into mechanical motion and sound.
A cell, wires, and a closed switch can light a lamp.

questions.explanation: A fuse can disconnect a circuit during a short circuit or overload.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes fuses and electrical safety from a nearby idea?

options.text: Series and parallel arrangements change how current paths and component behaviour are distributed.
A fuse is a safety device, not a switch for routine operation.
Energy use depends on both appliance power and the time it operates.
A closed conducting path is necessary; merely placing components nearby is not enough.

questions.explanation: A fuse is a safety device, not a switch for routine operation.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of fuses and electrical safety?

options.text: Switch off an appliance when it is not needed and use an efficient option.
Choose parallel branches when independent operation of lamps is needed.
Use the correct fuse rating and never replace a fuse with wire.
Trace the complete path from one terminal of the cell and back to the other.

questions.explanation: Use the correct fuse rating and never replace a fuse with wire.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about fuses and electrical safety?

options.text: A circuit with a gap cannot keep the lamp glowing.
Adding more lamps in series does not usually make every lamp brighter.
An appliance that is switched on is using energy even if the useful output is small.
A thicker wire in place of a fuse removes protection rather than improving it.

questions.explanation: A thicker wire in place of a fuse removes protection rather than improving it.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes series and parallel circuits in electricity: magnetic and heating effects?

options.text: Electrical appliances transfer electrical energy into useful forms such as light, heat, sound, or motion.
In a series circuit components share one path, while a parallel circuit provides separate branches.
An electric circuit is a complete conducting path through which electric current can flow.
Cells, switches, wires, and loads perform different roles in an electric circuit.

questions.explanation: In a series circuit components share one path, while a parallel circuit provides separate branches.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with series and parallel circuits?

options.text: A cell, wires, and a closed switch can light a lamp.
A mixer converts electrical energy mainly into mechanical motion and sound.
Two lamps in parallel can continue to receive a path if one branch opens.
A switch controls the path while a lamp changes electrical energy into light and heat.

questions.explanation: Two lamps in parallel can continue to receive a path if one branch opens.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes series and parallel circuits from a nearby idea?

options.text: A source supplies energy, a conductor provides a path, and a load uses energy.
Energy use depends on both appliance power and the time it operates.
A closed conducting path is necessary; merely placing components nearby is not enough.
Series and parallel arrangements change how current paths and component behaviour are distributed.

questions.explanation: Series and parallel arrangements change how current paths and component behaviour are distributed.

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of series and parallel circuits?

options.text: Choose parallel branches when independent operation of lamps is needed.
Switch off an appliance when it is not needed and use an efficient option.
Trace the complete path from one terminal of the cell and back to the other.
Represent a circuit using standard symbols before assembling it.

questions.explanation: Choose parallel branches when independent operation of lamps is needed.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes electrical energy use in electricity: magnetic and heating effects?

options.text: Conductors allow electric current to pass relatively easily, while insulators resist its flow.
An electric circuit is a complete conducting path through which electric current can flow.
Cells, switches, wires, and loads perform different roles in an electric circuit.
Electrical appliances transfer electrical energy into useful forms such as light, heat, sound, or motion.

questions.explanation: Electrical appliances transfer electrical energy into useful forms such as light, heat, sound, or motion.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with electrical energy use?

options.text: A mixer converts electrical energy mainly into mechanical motion and sound.
A cell, wires, and a closed switch can light a lamp.
A switch controls the path while a lamp changes electrical energy into light and heat.
Copper wire conducts, while plastic covering helps insulate it.

questions.explanation: A mixer converts electrical energy mainly into mechanical motion and sound.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 4; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes electrical energy use from a nearby idea?

options.text: A closed conducting path is necessary; merely placing components nearby is not enough.
Energy use depends on both appliance power and the time it operates.
A source supplies energy, a conductor provides a path, and a load uses energy.
The same object can have conducting and insulating parts for different safety roles.

Trace the complete path from one terminal of the cell and back to the other.

Test an unknown material only with a low-voltage classroom circuit.

An appliance that is switched on is using energy even if the useful output is small.
